title: Marathon 42K Training Plan 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Create Your Marathon Training Plan (42K) by Experience Level
description: Personalized marathon training plan (42K) calculator by experience level. Free tool for beginners to advanced runners. Get your training schedule.
intro: A marathon training plan calculates how many weeks of structured preparation you need to safely complete 42.195 km (26.2 miles) based on your current experience level. The core formula balances the 10% weekly mileage increase rule against your starting base fitness, peak weekly volume (typically 50–100 km/week), and the mandatory taper period (2–3 weeks) before race day. Beginners typically need 20–24 weeks, intermediate runners 16–18 weeks, and advanced athletes 12–16 weeks. Use this calculator before registering for a race to confirm you have enough time to train safely.
keyTakeaway: **Total Training Weeks = Base-Building Weeks + Peak Weeks + Taper (2–3 weeks)**. Example: an Intermediate runner starting at 30 km/week targeting 70 km/week peak needs ~14 build weeks + 2 peak weeks + 3 taper = **18–19 weeks total**.
useCases: A first-time marathoner with 6 months until race day wants to know if they have enough time to train from scratch (running 0–15 km/week).
An intermediate runner who completed a half-marathon (21K) in under 2 hours needs to know their 18-week peak mileage target and long-run schedule.
An advanced runner targeting a Boston Qualifier (sub-3:05 for men 18–34) needs to calculate weekly km peaks (90–110 km/week) and periodization blocks.
A runner returning after injury needs to calculate a modified 20-week plan with a conservative 5–7% weekly increase instead of the standard 10%.
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The number of training weeks is derived from three components: **base-building phase**, **peak/quality phase**, and **taper phase**.

```
Total Weeks = ceil((Peak_km - Current_km) / (Current_km × 0.10)) + Peak_Weeks + Taper_Weeks

Where:
  Peak_km        = target peak weekly mileage by experience level
  Current_km     = runner's current weekly mileage
  0.10           = maximum safe weekly increase (10% Rule, widely cited in sports medicine)
  Peak_Weeks     = 2–4 weeks at peak volume before taper
  Taper_Weeks    = 2–3 weeks of progressive mileage reduction (typically 20–30% cut per week)
```

**Example — Intermediate Runner:**
- Current weekly km: 35 km
- Target peak weekly km: 70 km
- Build weeks: ceil((70 − 35) / (35 × 0.10)) = ceil(35 / 3.5) = **10 weeks**
- Peak weeks: 3
- Taper: 3
- **Total: 16 weeks** (round up to 18 for rest/recovery weeks built in)

---

## Reference Table

| Experience Level | Current Weekly km | Peak Weekly km | Long Run Peak | Total Plan Weeks | Taper Weeks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Beginner** | 0–20 km | 50–55 km | 29–32 km | 20–24 weeks | 3 weeks |
| **Intermediate** | 25–45 km | 65–75 km | 32–35 km | 16–18 weeks | 3 weeks |
| **Advanced** | 50–70 km | 85–100 km | 35–38 km | 12–16 weeks | 2–3 weeks |
| **Elite / BQ Attempt** | 80–100 km | 110–130 km | 38–40 km | 12–14 weeks | 2 weeks |

*Long run peak is capped at ~35 km (22 miles) for most plans — running the full 42K before race day increases injury risk with minimal additional benefit.*

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — Complete Beginner (24-week plan)
Maria runs 10 km/week and wants to finish her first marathon. Target peak: 52 km/week.
- Build phase: ceil((52 − 10) / (10 × 0.10)) = ceil(42 / 1) = 42 weeks if strictly linear → **compressed to 20 weeks** with two 5-km weekly jumps early on and cutback weeks every 3rd week.
- Long run progresses: 12 → 16 → 19 → 22 → 25 → 29 → 32 km → taper.
- **Total: 24 weeks recommended.**

### Case 2 — Intermediate Runner (18-week plan)
James runs 35 km/week and has finished a half-marathon. Target peak: 70 km/week.
- Build: 10 progressive weeks, cutback every 3rd week.
- Long runs peak at 34 km in week 15.
- Taper: weeks 16–18 at 55 km → 42 km → 28 km.
- **Total: 18 weeks.**

### Case 3 — Advanced Runner Targeting Sub-3:30 (16-week plan)
Sarah runs 60 km/week consistently. Target peak: 90 km/week.
- Build: ceil((90 − 60) / 6) = 5 build weeks, then 8 quality/peak weeks with tempo runs and marathon-pace long runs.
- Long run peak: 38 km in week 13.
- Taper: 2.5 weeks.
- **Total: 16 weeks.**

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Skipping the taper** — Many runners fear losing fitness and keep high mileage the final 2 weeks. Studies show a 3-week taper with 20–30% weekly reduction improves race performance by 2–3% (Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research). Skipping taper leads to fatigue on race day.

2. **Ignoring the 10% Rule** — Increasing weekly mileage by more than 10% per week is the #1 cause of overuse injuries (stress fractures, shin splints, IT band syndrome). Even experienced runners must respect this ceiling when building a new base.

3. **Running the full 42K in training** — The long run should cap at 32–35 km. Running the full marathon distance before race day depletes glycogen stores, increases injury risk, and requires 3+ weeks of recovery — destroying your taper.

4. **Choosing too short a plan** — Selecting a 12-week plan when you need 20 weeks forces dangerous mileage jumps. Always calculate your required weeks *before* registering, not after.

5. **Not accounting for cutback weeks** — Every 3rd or 4th week should be a recovery week at 60–70% of the previous week's mileage. Failing to include these means accumulated fatigue will plateau or reverse fitness gains.

6. **Starting peak mileage too early** — Reaching 90+ km/week in week 4 of a 16-week plan and staying there causes burnout and injury. Peak volume should occur only 2–4 weeks before the taper begins.

---

faq.q: How many weeks does a beginner need to train for a marathon?
faq.a: Most sports medicine guidelines recommend 20–24 weeks for beginners starting from under 20 km/week. This allows time to safely build to a 50–55 km/week peak using the 10% weekly mileage increase rule, while including 6–8 cutback weeks and a 3-week taper. Rushing into a 12- or 16-week plan from zero base significantly raises injury risk.

faq.q: What is the 10% Rule and why does it matter?
faq.a: The 10% Rule states you should never increase your total weekly mileage by more than 10% from one week to the next. This guideline, widely cited in running medicine literature and endorsed by organizations like the American College of Sports Medicine, helps prevent overuse injuries such as stress fractures, plantar fasciitis, and IT band syndrome — the most common marathon training injuries.

faq.q: What is the longest run I should do before the marathon?
faq.a: For most runners (beginner to advanced), the longest training run should be 29–35 km (18–22 miles), done 2–3 weeks before race day. Running the full 42.195 km in training is counterproductive: it requires 3+ weeks of recovery, depletes muscle glycogen stores, and increases injury risk without meaningful additional fitness benefit.

faq.q: How does the taper work and how long should it be?
faq.a: The taper is a planned reduction in training volume (not intensity) in the final 2–3 weeks before race day. A standard taper cuts weekly mileage by roughly 20–30% each week: for example, 70 km → 52 km → 38 km → race week (~20 km). Research published in sports physiology journals shows a proper 3-week taper can improve race performance by 2–3% through muscle glycogen supercompensation and neuromuscular recovery.

faq.q: What peak weekly mileage should I target by experience level?
faq.a: Reference targets: Beginners aim for 50–55 km/week peak; Intermediate runners (with a half-marathon base) target 65–75 km/week; Advanced runners aiming for sub-3:30 should reach 85–100 km/week; and Boston Qualifier attempts typically require 100–130 km/week peak. These ranges are consistent with plans from Hal Higdon, Jack Daniels' Running Formula, and Pete Pfitzinger's 'Advanced Marathoning'.

faq.q: Can I use an 18-week plan if I have 24 weeks until my race?
faq.a: Yes — it's actually recommended. If you have more time than your plan requires, spend the extra weeks building your base mileage before starting the formal 18-week plan. For example, with 24 weeks available and an 18-week plan, use the first 6 weeks to increase your weekly km by 10% each week. This gives you a stronger base, reduces injury risk, and often leads to better race-day performance.

faq.q: What's a Boston Qualifying time and what training does it require?
faq.a: Boston Qualifying (BQ) standards set by the Boston Athletic Association (BAA) vary by age and gender. For men aged 18–34, the standard is sub-3:05:00; for women 18–34, sub-3:35:00. Achieving a BQ typically requires peak weekly mileage of 100–130 km, tempo runs at marathon pace (e.g., 4:22/km for a 3:05 finish), and 16–20 weeks of structured training. Check current BAA standards at baa.org as they are updated periodically.

faq.q: How do cutback weeks work in a marathon plan?
faq.a: Cutback weeks (also called recovery weeks) reduce total weekly mileage to 60–70% of the previous week's volume, typically every 3rd or 4th week. For example: Week 1: 50 km, Week 2: 55 km, Week 3: 60 km, Week 4 (cutback): 40 km. These weeks allow connective tissue, bones, and muscles to absorb training stress. Skipping cutback weeks is one of the most common causes of overtraining syndrome and DNS (Did Not Start) on race day.

faq.q: Is there a difference between km and miles in training plans?
faq.a: Yes — most US-based plans (Hal Higdon, NYRR) are written in miles, while European and international plans use kilometers. 1 mile = 1.609 km. A standard 18-week intermediate Hal Higdon plan peaks at ~45 miles/week = ~72 km/week. When comparing plans, always confirm the unit to avoid accidentally under- or over-training by ~60%.

sources.name: American College of Sports Medicine — Physical Activity Guidelines
NIH — National Library of Medicine: Overuse Injuries in Runners
Boston Athletic Association — Boston Marathon Qualifying Standards
